stem:
A 60-foot-tall office building and a flagpole on its roof both cast shadows on the ground on the same side of the building. The sun's angle of elevation is 53°. The tip of the flagpole's shadow falls 57 feet from the base of the building, measured along level ground. The building has 4 stories and was constructed with steel framing. What is the height, in feet, of the flagpole?

(tan 53° = 4/3)
explanation: Step 1: Find the length of the shadow cast by the building alone. The sun's angle of elevation is 53°, and the building is 60 feet tall. Using tan 53° = building height / shadow length: 4/3 = 60 / shadow, so shadow = 60 × (3/4) = 45 feet. Step 2: Determine the additional shadow caused by the flagpole. The total shadow extends 57 feet from the building's base, so the extra shadow beyond the building's own shadow is 57 − 45 = 12 feet. Step 3: Find the flagpole height. This extra 12 feet of shadow is produced by the flagpole at the same sun angle. Using tan 53° = flagpole height / 12: flagpole height = 12 × (4/3) = 16 feet. The number of stories and the construction material are extra information not needed. Verification: total height = 60 + 16 = 76 feet, total shadow = 76 × (3/4) = 57 feet, which matches.

distractorRationale.A: Finds the extra shadow length of 12 ft correctly but uses the reciprocal of tan 53° to compute the flagpole height: 12 × (3/4) = 9
distractorRationale.B: Computes the total height of building plus flagpole (57 × 4/3 = 76) but fails to subtract the building height to isolate the flagpole = 76
distractorRationale.C: Computes the extra shadow length (57 − 45 = 12) but reports this shadow length as the flagpole height instead of using it in the tangent equation = 12
distractorRationale.D: Correct: building shadow = 60 × (3/4) = 45, extra shadow = 57 − 45 = 12, flagpole = 12 × (4/3) = 16

imageDescription: A side-view diagram showing a tall building on the left with a flagpole on its roof. The building height is labeled 60 ft. Diagonal dashed lines represent sun rays at a 53° angle of elevation, casting shadows to the right on level ground. One ray touches the top of the flagpole and hits the ground 57 ft from the building base. The shadow of the building alone (45 ft) and the extra shadow from the flagpole (12 ft) are indicated along the ground. The flagpole height is marked with a question mark.
